Contact

Supporting your freedom to

THINK . GROW . LIVE — The House of Manna, a Baltimore food security, youth education, and community empowerment nonprofit

The House of Manna is a faith-inspired, publicly charitable nonprofit combining food security, entrepreneurship education, and community empowerment into one integrated model — helping individuals and families move beyond survival and toward possibility.

Stay in the loop

One ecosystem. Three ways we serve.

The Academy is our educational engine — exposing students and participants to entrepreneurship as a pathway toward economic empowerment.

The Pantry delivers our most immediate impact, providing food assistance in a way that protects dignity and reduces stress.

Mobile Distribution is where we're headed next — extending our reach further into the communities that need us most.

Scroll

“When people are worried about feeding their families, nothing else matters.”

Chad Parker · Founder
Explore ↗

The
Academy

Entrepreneurship education that turns participants into owners, not just employees — a pathway toward real economic independence.

Explore ↗

The
Pantry

Immediate food assistance delivered in a way that protects dignity, reduces stress, and never feels like charity.

Five pillars.
One mission.

Food security comes first — because when a family is worried about their next meal, nothing else can get their full attention. Entrepreneurship, sustainability, community empowerment, and hope build on that foundation.

01

Food Security

No family should have to choose between rent and groceries. Immediate relief comes first, delivered in a way that protects dignity.

02

Entrepreneurship

Whether someone pursues a business, a trade, or traditional employment, exposure to ownership opens a door that most never had access to.

03

Sustainability

We build programs designed to outlast a single grant cycle or election year — support that's still here next year, and the year after.

04

Community Empowerment

The people closest to the problem are closest to the solution. We build alongside the community, not on its behalf.

05

Hope for the Future

Restoring hope by demonstrating that opportunity exists, resources are available, and communities care.

Six phases of growth

From a single pilot pantry to a multi-state movement — here's the roadmap, one phase at a time. Tap any phase to see the goals behind it.

Timeline
Months 1–6
Target
25–50 families / month
Funding Focus
Founder & Founding Funding Partners

Many organizations make the mistake of serving more families than they can consistently support. This phase is about building awareness, establishing volunteer processes, and refining operations — success here is measured by consistency, not scale.

Timeline
Months 6–18
Target
100+ families / month
Funding Focus
Hidden Gems, Bloom Studio, Audaption, local sponsors, Maryland grants

Distribution moves to bi-weekly as volunteer participation expands, sponsor relationships develop, and food purchasing capacity increases. House of Manna begins applying for state-level grants and strengthening relationships with churches, schools, and local nonprofits.

Timeline
Years 2–3
Target
250+ families / month
Funding Focus
Funding Partners, corporate sponsors, individual donors, state & national grants

At this stage, House of Manna becomes a recognized community resource capable of serving hundreds of families every month — roughly 3,000 families served annually at full pace.

$10,000–$15,000Estimated monthly need to feed 250+ families
Timeline
Years 3–4
Target
New underserved communities
Funding Focus
Sustained Pantry & Academy funding base

House of Manna Mobile removes transportation barriers by bringing distribution directly into communities where access remains limited — initially targeting Belair-Edison, Broadway East, Oliver, Madison-Eastend, and Sandtown-Winchester through partnerships with churches, schools, and community centers.

Timeline
Years 4–5
Target
Glen Burnie & surrounding suburbs
Funding Focus
Proven Baltimore funding model

Following successful implementation within Baltimore, House of Manna expands into surrounding suburban communities — starting with Glen Burnie, where existing relationships and volunteer potential already exist, then Brooklyn Park, Dundalk, Essex, and Lansdowne.

Timeline
Years 5–10
Target
Chicago & beyond
Funding Focus
Diversified multi-market funding partners

The long-term vision extends beyond Maryland. Chicago represents the most likely first multi-state expansion, given House of Manna's deep personal, family, faith, and community connections there. No new location launches until the Academy, Pantry, reporting systems, and funding sources are proven and sustainable.

Targeting food desert areas

Where The House of Manna will focus outreach, pantry access, and community support.

Loading the Baltimore outreach map…
Existing AccessCurrent pantry and partner sites.
Underserved AreaLimited grocery access and high need.
Priority Outreach ZoneTargeted areas for expanded support.

How we prioritize

Limited Grocery Access

Areas with few or no full-service grocery stores within a close distance.

High Family Need

Communities with higher rates of food insecurity, child poverty, and housing challenges.

Strong Partnership Potential

Opportunities to collaborate with churches, schools, and community organizations to drive lasting impact.

Faith in action.  Food for today. Hope for tomorrow.

Measured by transformation.

Success will not be measured solely by dollars raised or food distributed. Success will be measured by transformation.

  • A family receiving support during a difficult season.
  • A student discovering a future career.
  • A volunteer finding purpose through service.
  • A sponsor investing in community change.
  • A neighborhood strengthened because House of Manna was present.

A faith-inspired, publicly charitable nonprofit combining food security, entrepreneurship education, and community empowerment into one integrated model.
